session.tool_result:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the app.py file. Let me compile the findings into a clear report with method categorization.
## Comprehensive Analysis of `app.py` Method Organization
Based on my thorough exploration of `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/tui/app.py` (1,685 lines), here's the complete categorization of methods by responsibility:
### 1. **Module-Level Utility Functions (Lines 51-94)** - `_OperationResult` dataclass (lines 51-57) - `_last_output_line(result)` (lines 60-62) - `_extract_learn_plan_number(result)` (lines 68-74) - `_build_github_url(plan_url, resource_type, number)` (lines 77-93)
### 2. **UI Composition and Lifecycle (Lines 96-235)** - `ErkDashApp` class definition with BINDINGS (lines 96-132) - `get_system_commands(screen)` (lines 134-146) — System command hiding logic - `__init__` constructor (lines 148-188) — Widget and state initialization - `_display_name_for_view(mode)` (lines 190-201) — View display naming - `compose()` (lines 203-217) — Layout creation - `on_mount()` (lines 219-235) — Widget reference setup and initial data load
### 3. **Data Loading and Caching (Lines 237-335)** - `_load_data()` async (lines 237-286) — Background thread data fetching with view mode snapshotting - `_update_table(rows, update_time, duration, ...)` (lines 288-334) — Cache update and table population with timing info - `_filter_rows_for_view(rows, mode)` static (lines 372-387) — View-mode row filtering (currently pass-through)
### 4. **Filter Management (Lines 336-371, 1231-1261)** - `_apply_filter_and_sort(rows)` (lines 336-370) — Main filter pipeline (objective → stack → text → sort) - `_apply_filter()` (lines 1231-1241) — Apply current filter to all rows and update table - `_exit_filter_mode()` (lines 1243-1260) — Clear filter state and restore table focus
... [120 lines omitted] ...

Perfect! I have enough information now. Let me compile a comprehensive report of the TUI subpackage organization patterns.
## TUI Subpackage Organization Report
Based on my exploration of the codebase, here's a comprehensive overview of the existing TUI subpackage organization patterns:
### **1. Subpackage Structure Pattern**
The TUI is organized into **specialized subpackages**, each with a clear responsibility:
``` src/erk/tui/ ├── __init__.py # Empty or minimal docstring only ├── app.py # Main Textual App (ErkDashApp) - NOT in a subpackage ├── commands/ # Command palette system │ ├── __init__.py # Minimal docstring │ ├── types.py # CommandDefinition, CommandContext, CommandCategory │ ├── provider.py # MainListCommandProvider, PlanCommandProvider classes │ └── registry.py # get_all_commands(), display generators, CATEGORY_EMOJI ├── data/ # Data layer │ ├── __init__.py # Minimal docstring │ └── types.py # PlanRowData, PlanFilters dataclasses ├── filtering/ # Filter logic │ ├── __init__.py # Minimal docstring │ ├── types.py # FilterState, FilterMode enums │ └── logic.py # filter_plans() pure function ├── sorting/ # Sort logic │ ├── __init__.py # Has helpful import guide in docstring │ ├── types.py # SortKey, SortState, BranchActivity │ └── logic.py # sort_plans() pure function
... [175 lines omitted] ...
- **One exception**: `sorting/__init__.py` has a helpful comment guide for imports:

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile a comprehensive exploration report.
## TUI Test Structure and ErkDashApp Import Analysis
I've completed a thorough exploration of the TUI test structure and ErkDashApp imports. Here's what I found:
### 1. Test Files in `tests/tui/`
**Main test structure:** - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/tests/tui/test_plan_table.py` - Unit tests for PlanDataTable widget and row conversion (650+ lines) - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/tests/tui/test_view_bar.py` - Tests for ViewBar widget - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/tests/tui/app/` - Main TUI app integration tests (subdirectory with 12+ test files): - `test_core.py` - Core app composition, data loading, navigation, refresh - `test_actions.py` - Action handlers and user interactions - `test_command_palette.py` - Command palette functionality - `test_streaming.py` - Streaming operations and output - `test_async_operations.py` - Async operations and background workers - `test_view_switching.py` - View mode switching (Plans, Learn, Objectives) - `test_app_filtering.py` - Filter application and state - `test_filter_mode.py` - Filter mode states - `test_operation_tracking.py` - Multi-operation tracking in status bar - `test_status_bar.py` - Status bar widget tests - `test_plan_detail_screen.py` - Plan detail modal screen - `test_plan_body_screen.py` - Plan body modal screen - `test_one_shot_prompt.py` - One-shot prompt modal - `test_utils.py` - Utility function tests - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/tests/tui/jsonl_viewer/` - Separate TUI for JSONL viewing
### 2. How Tests Import ErkDashApp
... [111 lines omitted] ...

session.tool_result: src/erk/tui/widgets/plan_table.py:17: from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp src/erk/tui/commands/provider.py:16: from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, PlanDetailScreen src/erk/tui/commands/provider.py:77: from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp src/erk/tui/commands/provider.py:170: from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp src/erk/tui/commands/provider.py:188: from erk.tui.app import PlanDetailScreen src/erk/tui/screens/plan_detail_screen.py:391: from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp src/erk/tui/screens/plan_detail_screen.py:627: from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp src/erk/cli/commands/pr/list_cmd.py:15:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp docs/learned/textual/testing.md:22:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, PlanDetailScreen tests/tui/app/test_async_operations.py:7:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, _extract_learn_plan_number, _OperationResult tests/tui/app/test_one_shot_prompt.py:5:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp tests/tui/app/test_command_palette.py:7:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, PlanDetailScreen tests/tui/app/test_filter_mode.py:5:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp tests/tui/app/test_view_switching.py:5:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp tests/tui/app/test_app_filtering.py:5:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp tests/tui/app/test_streaming.py:7:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, PlanDetailScreen tests/tui/app/test_core.py:5:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, HelpScreen tests/tui/app/test_actions.py:5:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp tests/tui/app/test_operation_tracking.py:7:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp tests/tui/app/test_utils.py:3:from erk.tui.app import _build_github_url tests/tui/app/test_plan_detail_screen.py:7:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, PlanDetailScreen tests/tui/app/test_plan_body_screen.py:6:from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp, PlanBodyScreen

1→--- 2→title: Tui Tripwires 3→read_when: 4→ - "working on tui code" 5→--- 6→ 7→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 8→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 9→<!-- Generated from tui/*.md frontmatter --> 10→ 11→# Tui Tripwires 12→ 13→Rules triggered by matching actions in code. 14→ 15→**accessing \_status_bar without null guard** → Read [TUI Streaming Output Patterns](streaming-output.md) first. Guard \_status_bar access with `if self._status_bar is not None:` — timing issue during widget lifecycle can cause AttributeError. 16→ 17→**accessing optional gateway fields without null checks in TUI event handlers** → Read [Modal Widget Embedding Pattern](modal-widget-embedding.md) first. Gateway fields like plan_body or objective_content may be None. Always check before accessing in event handlers. 18→ 19→**adding a CLI flag that affects behavior without checking TUI command palette** → Read [TUI Command Registration](tui-command-registration.md) first. TUI command palette generates shell commands via src/erk/tui/commands/registry.py. When adding CLI flags that change behavior, check if TUI-generated commands need the flag too. 20→ 21→**adding a DataTable column with add_column(key=...)** → Read [TUI Architecture Overview](architecture.md) first. Column key is a data binding contract — must match data field name. Silent failure when mismatched. 22→ 23→**adding a column to PlanDataTable without updating make_plan_row** → Read [Column Addition Pattern](column-addition-pattern.md) first. Column additions require 5 coordinated changes. See column-addition-pattern.md for the complete checklist. 24→ 25→**adding a command without an availability predicate** → Read [TUI Command Architecture](action-inventory.md) first. Every command needs an is_available predicate based on PlanRowData field presence. Commands without predicates appear when they can't execute. 26→ 27→**adding a field to PlanRowData without updating make_plan_row** → Read [TUI Data Contract](data-contract.md) first. The fake's make_plan_row() helper must stay in sync. Add the new field with a sensible default there too, or all TUI tests will break. 28→ 29→**adding a new TUI command without updating all 3 places** → Read [TUI Command Registration](tui-command-registration.md) first. TUI commands require 3-place coordination: registry definition, display formatter, and action inventory. See tui-command-registration.md. 30→
... [100 lines omitted] ...
31→**adding a new ViewMode without updating VIEW_CONFIGS** → Read [TUI View Switching](view-switching.md) first. Every ViewMode must have a corresponding ViewConfig in VIEW_CONFIGS. Missing configs cause KeyError at runtime. 39→**adding a subprocess call to the TUI without stderr inspection** → Read [TUI Subprocess Feedback Patterns](subprocess-feedback.md) first. TUI subprocess calls should inspect stderr for known success/failure markers. See subprocess-feedback.md for the pattern. 59→**calling start_operation() without a matching finish_operation() in the error path** → Read [TUI Multi-Operation Tracking](multi-operation-tracking.md) first. Always call finish_operation() in both success and error paths. Use try/finally or explicit error handling. Missing finish calls leave ghost operations in the status bar. 69→**constructing PlanFilters without copying all fields from existing filters** → Read [TUI Data Contract](data-contract.md) first. All fields must be explicitly copied in \_load_data() PlanFilters construction. Missing fields (like creator) cause silent filtering failures. 83→**extending PlanDataProvider ABC** → Read [TUI Architecture Overview](architecture.md) first. Requires 3-file update: abc.py + real.py + fake.py. Fake must initialize new dict in **init**. Missing init causes AttributeError at test time. 109→**removing a field from a frozen dataclass** → Read [Frozen Dataclass Field Management](frozen-dataclass-field-management.md) first. Grep for the class name across ALL constructor sites. Frozen dataclasses have 5+ places to update: field definition, real provider, fake provider, test helpers, and filtering/display logic. Missing one causes runtime TypeError.

session.tool_result:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me design the plan.
---
# Plan: Break Up `src/erk/tui/app.py` Into a Well-Organized Subpackage Structure
## Analysis Summary
**Current state**: `app.py` is 1686 lines with ~90 methods on `ErkDashApp` spanning 12 responsibility groups. It is the single largest file in the TUI package.
**Key constraints uncovered during exploration**:
1. **No existing mixin pattern** in the codebase -- introducing mixins would be a new pattern. 2. **Tests import from `erk.tui.app`** extensively: `ErkDashApp`, `_build_github_url`, `_extract_learn_plan_number`, `_OperationResult`. Some tests also import screen classes (`PlanDetailScreen`, `HelpScreen`, `PlanBodyScreen`) via `erk.tui.app` because app.py imports them at the top level and tests happened to reference them there. 3. **The `commands/provider.py`** accesses `app._get_selected_row()`, `app._view_mode`, `app._cmux_integration`, and `app.execute_palette_command()` -- these must remain public-ish on the class. 4. **Textual's `@work(thread=True)`** decorator and `call_from_thread()` pattern requires methods to be on the App class or have access to it. 5. **Existing subpackage convention**: `types.py` for frozen dataclasses/enums, `logic.py` for pure functions, empty `__init__.py`, absolute imports. The `filtering/`, `sorting/`, `views/`, `commands/`, `data/` subpackages all follow this pattern.
## Recommended Approach: Option C (Module-level functions + Mixin for action groups)
After careful analysis, I recommend **Option C: Module-level functions for extractable logic, plus targeted mixins for the `action_*` and `@work` method groups that must stay on the class**.
**Why not pure mixins (Option A)?** Mixins are a new pattern for this codebase. Using them everywhere would create many classes that all share `self` state in a confusing way. However, the Textual framework fundamentally requires `action_*` methods and `@on` event handlers to be on the class. A targeted use of mixins for these groups is the pragmatic solution.
**Why not pure delegates (Option B)?** The `@work(thread=True)` methods and `call_from_thread()` pattern require tight coupling to the App instance. Delegates would need the app reference passed in, and every `call_from_thread(self.notify, ...)` would become awkward. The forwarding cost is too high for 350+ lines of async workers.
**Why not full partial-class (Option D)?** This is essentially the same as Option A but less explicit about the inheritance pattern.
**The hybrid approach**: Extract pure logic into standalone module functions (matching the existing `filtering/logic.py` and `sorting/logic.py` pattern), and use a small number of focused mixin classes for the method groups that must remain on the App class.
... [301 lines omitted] ...
session.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive plan from the Plan agent. Let me review it and refine it before writing the final plan. Let me also check a few things I want to clarify.
A key question: should I use mixins at all? The user asked to "break up" the file. Mixins are one approach but they can be controversial. Let me think about what makes the most sense.
Looking at the analysis: - The pure functions/types extraction is clearly good (operations/types.py, operations/logic.py) - The mixin approach for action methods is pragmatic given Textual's constraints - The event handlers mixin makes sense
- The background workers mixin also makes sense given that's 350 lines of repetitive code
The Plan agent's approach is solid. Since the user specifically wants to reorganize the file into a better-structured subpackage, I should focus on clarifying a few implementation details with them—like whether mixins feel right for this codebase, how to handle the underscore prefix on internal types when they move, and what other naming conventions to apply.
Actually, I think the mixin strategy is well-justified given Textual's constraints, so I'll just write out the plan rather than asking preliminary questions. Let me draft that now.
session.assistant: Good, I have thorough analysis from all three agents. Let me write the plan.

session.tool_result:
PreToolUse:ExitPlanMode hook error: [command -v erk >/dev/null 2>&1 || exit 0; ERK_HOOK_ID=exit-plan-mode-hook erk exec exit-plan-mode-hook]: DISPLAY PLAN: Before asking the question below, read the plan file and display its contents to the user with proper markdown formatting: /Users/schrockn/.claude/plans/keen-prancing-cherny.md
PLAN SAVE PROMPT
A plan exists for this session but has not been saved.
Use AskUserQuestion to ask the user: question: "📋 Plan: Break Up `src/erk/tui/app.py` Into Organized Subpackage Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)\n\nWhat would you like to do with this plan?" header: "br:master"
IMPORTANT: Present options in this exact order: 1. "Create a plan PR" (Recommended) - Create a draft PR with the plan and stop. Does NOT proceed to implementation. 2. "Skip PR and implement here" - Skip creating a PR, implement directly in current worktree (for small PR iterations that don't need PR tracking). 3. "View/Edit the plan" - Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.
⚠️ WARNING: Currently on 'master'. We strongly discourage editing directly on the trunk branch. Consider saving the plan and implementing in a dedicated worktree instead.
If user chooses 'Create a plan PR': 1. Run /erk:plan-save 2. STOP - Do NOT call ExitPlanMode. The plan-save command handles everything. Stay in plan mode and let the user exit manually if desired.
If user chooses 'Skip PR and implement here': 1. Create implement-now marker (skip PR creation): erk exec marker create --session-id be4d0d85-bc1a-435f-ae7f-2d47a33939ca \ exit-plan-mode-hook.implement-now 2. Call ExitPlanMode 3. After exiting plan mode, implement the changes directly
... [7 lines omitted] ...
session.thinking: The hook is intercepting ExitPlanMode. I need to display the plan and ask the user what they want to do with it using the AskUserQuestion tool, following the exact format specified.
session.assistant:
Here's the plan:
# Plan: Break Up `src/erk/tui/app.py` Into Organized Subpackage
## Context
`app.py` is 1686 lines with ~90 methods covering 12 distinct responsibility areas. The rest of the TUI is well-organized into focused subpackages, but the main app class carries everything in a single file. Goal: reduce to ~350-line orchestrator.
## Approach: Module Functions + Targeted Mixins
**Why mixins?** Textual requires `action_*` and `@on(...)` handlers on the App class — can't move to standalone modules. Mixins split the class across files while keeping all methods accessible as `self.action_*()`.
**Why module functions too?** Pure utilities (`_build_github_url`, `_OperationResult`, etc.) follow the existing `filtering/logic.py` and `sorting/types.py` pattern.
## Proposed Structure
``` src/erk/tui/ ├── app.py # ~350 lines (lifecycle, data loading, view switching) ├── operations/ # Background operation infrastructure │ ├── types.py # OperationResult dataclass │ ├── logic.py # Pure helpers (last_output_line, extract_learn_plan_number, build_github_url) │ ├── streaming.py # StreamingOperationsMixin (4 methods) │ └── workers.py # BackgroundWorkersMixin (12 @work methods, ~350 lines) ├── actions/ # Keyboard action handlers │ ├── navigation.py # NavigationActionsMixin (~175 lines) │ ├── filter_actions.py # FilterActionsMixin (~130 lines) │ └── palette.py # PaletteActionsMixin (~230 lines) ├── events.py # EventHandlersMixin (10 @on handlers, ~90 lines) ```
## 6-Step Implementation Sequence
1. Extract `operations/` (types + logic) — pure functions, lowest risk 2. Extract `operations/streaming.py` — StreamingOperationsMixin 3. Extract `operations/workers.py` — BackgroundWorkersMixin (biggest extraction) 4. Extract `events.py` — EventHandlersMixin 5. Extract `actions/` — 3 mixins for navigation, filters, palette 6. Clean up test imports (screens via canonical locations)
Tests run after each step. `ErkDashApp` stays importable from `erk.tui.app`.

tool_use.name: AskUserQuestion
tool_use.param: [{'question': 'Plan: Break Up `src/erk/tui/app.py` Into Organized Subpackage\nCurrent cont...[truncated 443 chars]...ion': 'Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

session.tool_result:
User has answered your questions: "Plan: Break Up `src/erk/tui/app.py` Into Organized Subpackage Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)
What would you like to do with this plan?"="Create a plan PR (Recommended)". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.
session.user:
# /erk:plan-save
Save the current session's plan to GitHub with session context.
## Usage
```bash /erk:plan-save # Standalone plan /erk:plan-save --plan-type=learn # Learn plan (erk-learn label) /erk:plan-save --objective=123 # Explicit objective link (overrides marker) ```
Objective linking: if a plan was created via `/erk:objective-plan`, the session's objective-context marker is read automatically by the save command. Use `--objective=<number>` to override the marker or provide the link when no marker exists (e.g., during replan).
## Plan Storage
The plan is saved using the configured backend:
- **Draft PR backend** (`PLAN_BACKEND = "draft_pr"`): Creates a branch, pushes a plan commit, and opens a draft PR. Plan content is in the PR body after the metadata separator. The JSON output contract includes `plan_number`, `plan_url`, `title`, `branch_name`, `plan_backend`.
## Agent Instructions
### Step 1: Parse Arguments
Check `` for the `--plan-type` and `--objective` flags:
``` If contains "--plan-type=<type>": - Extract the type (standard or learn) - Store as PLAN_TYPE variable - Set PLAN_TYPE_FLAG to "--plan-type=<type>" Else: - Set PLAN_TYPE_FLAG to empty string
If contains "--objective=<number>": - Extract the number - Set OBJECTIVE_FLAG to "--objective=<number>" Else: - Set OBJECTIVE_FLAG to empty string ```
### Step 2: Generate Branch Slug
Before saving, generate a branch slug from the plan title. Read the plan title from the plan file you wrote in plan mode (the first `# ` heading).
Generate a branch slug from the title:
- 2-4 hyphenated lowercase words, max 30 characters - Capture distinctive essence, drop filler words (the, a, for, implementation, plan) - Prefer action verbs: add, fix, refactor, update, consolidate, extract, migrate - Examples: "fix-auth-session", "add-plan-validation", "refactor-gateway-abc"
Store the result as `BRANCH_SLUG`.
### Step 3: Generate Plan Summary
Write a concise 2-3 sentence summary of the plan. This summary will be visible at the top of the PR description (above the collapsed full plan).
Guidelines:
- Focus on WHAT the plan does and WHY - Do not repeat the title - Plain text, no markdown headers or formatting - Avoid special shell characters (backticks, dollar signs) - Store as PLAN_SUMMARY
### Step 4: Run Save Command
Run this command with the session ID, branch slug, summary, and optional flags:
```bash erk exec plan-save --format json --session-id="be4d0d85-bc1a-435f-ae7f-2d47a33939ca" --branch-slug="${BRANCH_SLUG}" --summary="${PLAN_SUMMARY}" ${PLAN_TYPE_FLAG} ${OBJECTIVE_FLAG} ```
Parse the JSON output to extract `plan_number` for verification in Step 5.
If the command fails, display the error and stop.
### Step 5: Verify Objective Link (if applicable)
**Only run this step if `objective_issue` is non-null in the JSON output from Step 4.**
Verify the objective link was saved correctly:
```bash erk exec get-plan-metadata <plan_number> objective_issue ```
Parse the JSON response:
- If `success: true` and `value` matches the expected objective number: verification passed - If `success: false` or value doesn't match: verification failed
**On verification success:**
Display: `Verified objective link: #<objective-number>`
**On verification failure:**
Display error and remediation steps:
``` ERROR: Objective link verification failed Expected objective: #<expected> Actual: <actual-or-null>
The plan was saved but without the correct objective link. Fix: Close draft PR #<plan_number>, ensure the objective-context marker exists, and re-run /erk:plan-save. ```
Exit without creating the plan-saved marker. The session continues so the user can retry.
### Step 6: Update Objective Roadmap (if objective linked)
**Only run this step if `objective_issue` was non-null in JSON output and verification passed.**
Update the objective's roadmap table to show that a plan has been created for this node:
1. **Read the roadmap node marker** to get the node ID:
```bash step_id=$(erk exec marker read --session-id "be4d0d85-bc1a-435f-ae7f-2d47a33939ca" roadmap-step) ```
If the marker doesn't exist (command fails), skip this step - the plan wasn't created via `objective-plan`.
2. **Update the roadmap table** using the dedicated command:
```bash erk exec update-objective-node <objective-issue> --node "$step_id" --pr "#<plan_number>" --status in_progress ```
This atomically fetches the objective body, finds the matching node row, sets the Status cell to `in-progress`, and writes the updated body back.
3. **Report the update:**
Display: `Updated objective #<objective-issue> roadmap: node <step_id> → plan #<plan_number>`
**Error handling:** If the roadmap update fails, warn but continue - the plan was saved successfully, just the roadmap tracking didn't update. The user can manually update the objective.
### Step 7: Display Results
**If JSON contains `skipped_duplicate: true`:**
Display: `Plan already saved as #<plan_number> (duplicate skipped)`
If `branch_name` is present in the JSON, display the same next-steps block as the success case below.
If `branch_name` is absent, display only: `View PR: <plan_url>`
Return immediately (skip Steps 5, 6 above if not already executed).
**Otherwise, on success**, display:
``` Plan "<title>" saved as draft PR #<plan_number> URL: <issue_url> ```
**Slot options block:**
The "OR exit Claude Code first" section should show both slot allocation options, with the recommended one listed first based on trunk detection:
If **on trunk = true**:
``` OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
New slot (recommended — you're on trunk): Local: erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Same slot: Local: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If **on trunk = false**:
``` OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
Same slot (recommended — you're in a slot): Local: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
New slot: Local: erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
**Next steps block:**
``` Next steps:
View PR: <plan_url>
In Claude Code: Dispatch to queue: /erk:pr-dispatch — Dispatch plan for remote agent implementation
OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of: Checkout: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If objective was verified, also display: `Verified objective link: #<objective-number>`
If the JSON output contains `slot_name` and `slot_objective_updated: true`, also display: `Slot objective updated: <slot_name> → #<objective-number>`
**Note:** Slot objective updates are handled automatically by `plan-save` when an objective is linked via the session marker - no separate command call needed.
On failure, display the error message and suggest:
- Checking that a plan exists (enter Plan mode and exit it first) - Verifying GitHub CLI authentication (`gh auth status`) - Checking network connectivity
## Session Tracking
After successfully saving a plan, the plan number is stored in a marker file that enables automatic plan updates in the same session.
**To read the saved plan number:**
```bash erk exec marker read --session-id "be4d0d85-bc1a-435f-ae7f-2d47a33939ca" plan-saved-issue ```
This returns the plan number (exit code 0) or exits with code 1 if no plan was saved in this session.
session.assistant:
Let me execute the plan save steps.
**Step 1: Parse Arguments** — No flags provided, so standard plan type and no objective link.
**Step 2: Generate Branch Slug**
From the plan title "Break Up `src/erk/tui/app.py` Into Organized Subpackage", the slug is: `refactor-tui-app-subpackage`
**Step 3: Generate Plan Summary**
Extracts the ~1700-line ErkDashApp class into focused mixins and module-level functions, following existing TUI subpackage conventions. Creates operations/ and actions/ subpackages for background workers and keyboard handlers, reducing app.py to a ~350-line orchestrator.
**Step 4: Run Save Command**

Save plan as draft PR via erk exec

Next steps:
View PR: https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/pull/8528
In Claude Code: Dispatch to queue: `/erk:pr-dispatch` — Dispatch plan for remote agent implementation
OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
New slot (recommended — you're on trunk): Local: `erk br co --new-slot --for-plan 8528` Implement: `source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan 8528 --script)" && erk implement --dangerous`
Same slot: Local: `erk br co --for-plan 8528` Implement: `source "$(erk br co --for-plan 8528 --script)" && erk implement --dangerous`
Dispatch to Queue: `erk pr dispatch 8528`
session.user:
# /erk:pr-dispatch
## Goal
Find the most recent plan created in this conversation and dispatch it for remote AI implementation via `erk pr dispatch`.
## What This Command Does
1. Search conversation for the last plan reference 2. Extract the plan number 3. Run `erk pr dispatch <plan_number>` to trigger remote implementation
## Finding the Plan
Search the conversation from bottom to top for these patterns (in priority order):
1. **Draft PR reference**: `saved as draft PR #<number>` or `draft PR #<number>` 2. **Pull request URL**: `https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/pull/<number>` 3. **Plan URL**: `https://github.com/.../pull/<number>` (legacy: `https://github.com/.../issues/<number>`)
Extract the plan number from the most recent match.
## Execution
Once you have the plan number, run:
```bash erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If the command succeeds, clear the session marker to allow creating new plans in this session:
```bash erk exec marker delete --session-id "be4d0d85-bc1a-435f-ae7f-2d47a33939ca" plan-saved-issue ```
Display the command output to the user. The `erk pr dispatch` command handles all validation (plan existence, labels, state).
## Error Cases
- **No plan found in conversation**: Report "No plan found in conversation. Run /erk:plan-save first to create a plan." - **erk pr dispatch fails**: Display the error output from the command (erk pr dispatch validates the plan)
session.assistant: The most recent plan is #8528. Let me dispatch it.
session.tool_result:
Checking GitHub authentication... Syncing trunk with remote... Resolving GitHub username... Validating 1 planned-PR plan(s)...
Validating PR #8528...
✓ All 1 plan(s) validated
#8528: [erk-plan] Plan: Break Up `src/erk/tui/app.py` Into Organized Subpackage
Dispatching PR #8528...
Fetching plan content... Checking out existing plan branch: plnd/refactor-tui-app-subpackag-03-01-0620 Cleaning up previous .erk/impl-context/ folder... Creating .erk/impl-context/ folder... ✓ Branch pushed to remote
Triggering workflow: plan-implement.yml Waiting for workflow run... (attempt 1/11) Waiting for workflow run... (attempt 2/11) ✓ Workflow triggered. ✓ Dispatch metadata written Posting queued event comment... ✓ Queued event comment posted
✓ 1 plan(s) dispatched successfully!
... [5 lines omitted] ...

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: Clear plan-saved marker for this session

session.assistant:
Plan #8528 has been dispatched for remote implementation.
- **Plan PR**: https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/pull/8528 - **Workflow run**: https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/actions/runs/22542375387
The remote agent will implement the refactoring — breaking `app.py` into the `operations/` and `actions/` subpackages with mixins.
session.user: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
